Context: Reproducing game bugs, in our case crash bugs in con-
5
tinuously evolving games like Minecraft, is a notoriously manual,
2
0  time-consuming, and challenging process to automate. Despite the
2 success of LLM-driven bug reproduction in other software domains,
 
r games, with their complex interactive environments, remain largely
a unaddressed.
Objective: This paper introduces BugCraft, a novel end-to-end
M
   framework designed to automate the reproduction of crash bugs in
5 Minecraft directly from user-submitted bug reports, addressing the
2 critical gap in automated game bug reproduction.
 
   BugCraft employs a two-stage approach: first, a Step

Synthesizer leverages LLMs andMinecraftWiki knowledge to trans-

form bug reports into high-quality, structured steps to reproduce
S
.  (S2R). Second, an Action Model, powered by a vision-based LLM
s agent (GPT-4o) and a custom macro API, executes these S2R steps
c
[ within Minecraft to trigger the reported crash. To facilitate evalua-
 
   tion, we introduce BugCraft-Bench, a curated dataset of Minecraft
1 crash bug reports.
v  Results: Evaluated on BugCraft-Bench, our framework success-
6  fully reproduced 30.23% of crash bugs end-to-end. The Step Syn-
3  thesizer demonstrated a 66.28% accuracy in generating correct bug
0
reproduction plans, highlighting its effectiveness in interpreting
0
and structuring bug report information.
2
.  Conclusion: BugCraft demonstrates the feasibility of automated
3 reproduction of crash bugs in complex game environments using
0 LLMs, opening promising avenues for game testing and develop-
5
ment. The framework and the BugCraft-Bench dataset pave the
2
: way for future research in automated game bug analysis and hold
v potential for generalization to other interactive game platforms.
i

Reproduction Using LLM Agents
ecraft
ncer Öztürk  Eray Tüzün
ring Department,  Computer Engineering Department,
niversity  Bilkent University
Turkey  Ankara, Turkey
.bilkent.edu.tr  eraytuzun@cs.bilkent.edu.tr

In the maintenance phase of the game development cycle, it is cru-
cial to provide updates that improve the game to maintain player re-
tention and enhance engagement [24]. These game updates should
also fix bugs for improved user experience. As a type of bug, crash
bugs are especially severe because they prevent the user from ex-
periencing the game. They are also difficult to fix, being the most
recurring bug type even after updates addressing the bug [17].
A primary source of information for these updates is the user-
1
submitted bug reports. A prime example is Mojira , the public
bug tracker for Minecraft developer Mojang Studios. Mojira hosts
more than 450,000 bug reports, with over half related to Minecraft:
2
Java Edition . However, many of these reports are of low quality,
often missing essential components like steps to reproduce (S2R),
expected outcomes, and can have mistakes in them [16, 25]. The
studies showing low-quality bug reports across numerous projects
suggest that Mojira could potentially face similar challenges.
Due to the excessive amount of labor involved, game testing is an
entire field in itself, and it is a notoriously manual process that takes
a significant amount of time [12]. Most games lack automated test
frameworks because implementing and generalizing such systems is
challenging. For example, Minecraft’s internal unit test framework
3
GameTest  lacks the tools to reproduce UI movement and precise
player interactions, only covering a part of what is a huge sandbox
game.
Looking at a more general usage, automated bug reproduction
systems have been developed [5]. With the advent of large language
models (LLMs) that are capable of tool calling (i.e., interacting with
external tools/APIs) [14] and code generation [4], the implementa-
tions of these systems have become more general. Similar works
[18] exist for reproducing bugs in Android apps through interact-
ing with the available GUI. These works are limited in interacting
with the game, which needs action taking in a complex environ-
ment. To our knowledge, there are no works for automating bug
reproduction in an open-ended game environment like Minecraft.
In this paper, we present BugCraft, an agentic bug reproduction
framework for Minecraft. BugCraft can take in the initial bug report,
synthesize high-quality steps to reproduce bugs alongside relevant
game data from external sources and reproduce them step by step
through scripting. In the end, our framework generates a log that
can reproduce the bug. In addition, due to the lack of a dataset
of crash bugs in a game, we present BugCraft-Bench, a crash bug
dataset collected through Mojira. Our dataset contains 86 manually
1https://bugs.mojang.com/issues
2https://www.minecraft.net/en-us/about-minecraft
3https://www.minecraft.net/en-us/creator/article/get-started-gametest-framework

lowed by an overview of the framework in Section 3. We define
our research questions in Section 3.3, evaluate the framework in
Section 4, and discuss our findings in Section 5. The paper mentions
the threats to validity in Section 6, and concludes with a summary
of contributions in Section 7.
2  Related Work
This section provides an overview of related topics, focusing on
bug reproduction in games and the development of agents powered
by LLMs. We also discuss similar research efforts relevant to our
framework.
2.1  Bug Reproduction in Games
Game testing is traditionally a manual and time-consuming process,
and the primary approach involves playtesting, in which testers
manually play the game to identify issues [12]. Automated unit
testing is often challenging for game developers due to the dynamic
nature of game code and the complexity involved in capturing game
states programmatically [11]. In continuous development cycles, it
becomes essential for developers to address existing bugs through
regular updates to improve the gameplay experience. However,
certain types of bugs, particularly crash bugs, are prone to reoc-
currence even after an update is released to fix them [17]. One
developer in a recent survey attributed this issue to limited or
non-available testing time, highlighting the constraints of manual
testing in the fast-paced, deadline-driven environment typical of
game development.
Wuji [23] represents the closest related work in automated game
testing, using deep reinforcement learning to explore the game
model to discover new bugs. However, Wuji’s capabilities are lim-
ited to predefined game areas and narrow action spaces. It requires
manual environment setup without the ability to follow specific
test prompts. Furthermore, Wuji operates in the domain of bug
finding, while our framework focuses on reproducing known bugs.
Our approach provides end-to-end bug reproduction capabilities,
starting from game initialization and covering specific scenarios,
enabling a more comprehensive approach to addressing reported
issues.

Yapağcı, Öztürk and Tüzün
2.2  Automated Bug Reproduction Using LLMs
Automated bug reproduction entails converting unstructured bug
reports into executable scripts reliably replicating the reported is-
sues. LLMs have shown considerable success in generating code,
particularly in creating unit tests [5] and enhancing bug report de-
tails [1]. Recent frameworks leveraging LLMs have been developed
to automate bug reproduction in mobile applications, particularly
within Android environments, by interacting with the GUI [18].
These frameworks enable a series of structured actions where the
LLMmakes decisions based on the bug report, GUI interactions, and
prior actions while additional contextual information is provided
as needed by the framework. Frameworks in [18] and [3] rely on
methods that convert the GUI into textual representations, allow-
ing the LLM to interact with and manipulate the interface through
text-based commands. Additionally, a study [6] incorporates visual
inputs, enhancing the LLM’s capacity to interpret and interact with
complex GUIs. This approach has achieved performance compara-
ble to other vision-based methods, highlighting the potential for
LLMs to effectively combine textual and visual data in automated
bug reproduction tasks.
2.3  LLM Agents
LLMs have shown great success at acting as artificial agents, which
are entities capable of perceiving their surroundings, making deci-
sions, and taking actions in response [20, 22]. The agent framework
involves equipping LLMs with tools, often in the form of APIs, to
expand their action space and enable them to interact effectively
with the environment. The most important part is that the LLMs
were able to do this mainly by utilizing in-context learning [2], alle-
viating the need to train specialized models for each in-domain task.
Moreover, LLMs have also shown to be successful at taking action
in an open-ended environment. This ability to act extends even to
games like Minecraft, where the Voyager framework leveraged an
LLM agent to achieve in-game progress milestones significantly
faster than previous methods [19].

Our experiment utilizes a custom dataset of Minecraft crash bug
reports, processed through a custom framework called BugCraft,
which operates in two main phases: creating the Steps to Repro-
duce (S2R) and executing the S2R (step clusters). Figure 3 provides
a high-level overview of our framework. The following sections
will elaborate on its components: we begin with the dataset cre-
ation process (subsection 3.1), followed by a brief introduction to
Bugcraft (subsection 3.2). We then describe the preprocessing of
a bug report (subsection 3.2.1), the Step Synthesizer component
(subsection 3.2.2), the Action Model component (subsection 3.2.3),
the general implementation details of the entire framework (sub-
section 3.2.4) and finally the evaluation setup (subsection 3.3).
3.1  Data Curation
To our knowledge, no readily available dataset of Minecraft bug
reports exists. Therefore, to quantitatively measure the efficiency
of our framework, we constructed our own dataset of crash bug
reports. Furthermore, we gathered data from the Minecraft Wiki to
enrich our framework with in-game information.

BugCraft: End-to-End Crash Bug Reproduction Using LLM Agents in Minecraft
Figure 2: Bug Report Codenamed MC-276621
3.1.1  BugCraft-Bench Dataset Curation. We built a custom dataset
consisting of 86 Minecraft: Java Edition crash bug reports to eval-
uate our framework. These reports were collected from Mojira,
Minecraft’s official bug tracker, utilizing Jira’s REST API. We em-
ployed a finegrained query to retrieve all reports classified under
the "Crash" category. Moreover, for each report, we gathered all as-
sociated comments, attachments, and external links (e.g., YouTube
links), which may contain relevant files or videos. These were iden-
tified using regular expressions (Regex).
In Figure 2, we present a sample bug report, identified as MC-
4
276621 , which was verified and resolved by Mojang in a subse-
quent snapshot. This report illustrates a bug that can be reproduced
through a specific combination of commands and UI interactions.
Additionally, another user, contributed steps-to-reproduce (S2R),
which were initially missing from the report. Importantly, the BR in
its final state lacks commands that would make reproducing the bug
easier. Furthermore, though not relevant to this particular report,
5
some bug reports, such as MC-275657 , contain an implicit assump-
tion that a version-specific setting is enabled without explicitly
stating it, creating ambiguity during reproduction.
Each report in the dataset was categorized according to its "Con-
firmation Status" label in Mojira, which includes four possible
states: "Confirmed," "Community Consensus," "Plausible," and "Un-
confirmed." We primarily focused on "Confirmed" and "Community
Consensus" reports, as these have been verified by the game’s devel-
oper, Mojang Studios, making them reliable ground truth instances.
To focus solely on singleplayer bugs, we excluded reports related to
servers, multiplayer interactions, and those requiring external files
loaded into the game (such as datapacks, worlds, or world setting
JSONs).
To ensure the quality and relevance of our dataset, we employed a
multi-stage filtration process, focusing specifically on single-player
crash bugs. We began by targeting the most recent 300 (limited the
size of the dataset because of resource constraints) crash bug reports
4https://bugs.mojang.com/browse/MC-276621
5https://bugs.mojang.com/browse/MC-275657

from Mojira’s database following our query results, spanning from
6
the most recent, MC-277967  (resolved on 05/Nov/24, affecting
7
version 24w44a), to MC-145102  (created on 19/Feb/19, affecting
version 19w08b), covering a total of 70 different game versions. We
then downloaded all associated data for each of these 300 reports,
including comments, attachments, and external links. Next, we
undertook amanual review, a crucial step that involved categorizing
each report based on several criteria. Our focus was to isolate single-
player crash bugs, so we excluded reports involving multiplayer
8
aspects like servers or Realms (a subscription based server service)  ,
those requiring interactions between different game versions, and
any that depended on external resources such as datapacks, custom
worlds, resource packs, or specific world settings. We also removed
reports related to issues outside the game itself or those that were
machine-dependent. Furthermore, reports were discarded if they
were considered low quality due to inadequate information or if
the final version differed so much from the initial draft that it could
be considered a new submission. This meticulous review resulted
in a refined set of 141 reports. Due to resource constraints and the
significant cost associated with the LLM API calls, we chose to
evaluate only a portion of the full dataset. As such, from the initial
pool of 141 reports, we randomly selected a subset of 100 to create
our final dataset.

This dataset was used for detailed analysis and
the evaluation of our framework.
9
3.1.2  Minecraft Wiki Dataset. We use the official Minecraft Wiki
site as our knowledge base. Since the base of the site used Medi-
aWiki, we are able to get the wiki’s text using the provided wiki
API. We pulled every page in the wiki that was not a redirect page.
We stored each page as a separate file and used fuzzy matching
with the page titles for retrieval. Using the wiki as our knowledge
base allows us to circumvent reliance on our underlying LLM’s
pre-training data.
3.2  BugCraft Framework
The BugCraft framework (Figure 3) is designed for automated bug
reproduction and comprises two core components: the Step Syn-
thesizer and the Action Model. These components work in tandem,
with the Step Synthesizer generating a sequence of steps and the
Action Model providing the executable actions, to transform raw,
unstructured bug report data into executable scripts for accurate
bug replication. We outline each component below.
3.2.1  Preprocessing. The initial stages of our process involve a cru-
cial preprocessing phase applied to the raw bug report data. This
phase begins with extracting the entirety of the textual content,
encompassing the very first submission of the bug report and all
subsequent comments. It’s important to understand that we specifi-
cally focus on these initial versions because they represent the most
challenging scenario for our framework. Specifically, we analyze
the state of the bug report as it was initially submitted by the user
before any modifications or refinements were made by the Mojira
team. These initial reports are often less structured, potentially con-
taining incomplete information or ambiguous language, reflecting

8https://www.minecraft.net/en-us/realms

(latest version)

report
Figure 3: BugCr
the user’s initial understanding and description of the issue. This
contrasts significantly with later, revised versions, which benefit
from the input and clarifications provided by experienced team
members. The core challenge, and our framework’s primary ob-
jective, is accurately interpreting and addressing these raw, initial
reports. Subsequent refinements often simplify the report, making
the issue easier to understand, but our focus is on the initial state
of the report created by the user.
While the Mojira API lacks a direct method for retrieving pre-
vious versions, it does provide access to the issue’s changelog, de-
tailing the history of each field. We developed a back-tracker script
to parse these fields and reconstruct the bug report at a specific
point in its changelog. Comments are not included in the changelog,
so we delete any comments that were added after the selected re-
construction step. To maximize the availability of information, the
back-tracker stops at the latest state of the BR that has not been
touched by a Mojira team member or a trusted individual (Users
with [Helper] role). Using Mojira’s role-based system, we specif-
ically target the last version prior to any changes made by users
with the roles [Mod], [Helper], or [Mojang].
3.2.2  Step Synthesizer. The Step Synthesizer component seen in
Figure 4 is responsible for converting the unstructured text of bug
reports into high-quality S2R steps, augmented with relevant exter-
nal information. For the entire step synthesizing process, we use
the same MLLM (Multimodal Large Language Model), which we
call the Step LLM. Further details regarding the prompts utilized
in the Step Synthesizer are provided in the reproduction package.
This process occurs in the following stages:
Knowledge Augmentation: Prior to generating the S2R steps,
we aim to identify relevant wiki pages. This process begins by
prompting the Step LLM with the bug report to perform named en-
tity recognition, resulting in a list of entities. Fuzzymatching is then
employed to compare this list against wiki page titles, yielding a set
of potentially relevant wiki page titles. Then, the Step LLM selects
the most relevant titles from the set. In addition to these specifically
identified pages, we incorporate generally relevant pages, such as
those pertaining to the bug report’s version, general game mechan-
ics, and commands. Once all titles are selected, the corresponding
pages are fetched, and individual analysis trajectories are generated
for each page. Inspired by the self-reasoning paper [21], which
generates a reasoning trajectory for each retrieved chunk, we adopt
a similar approach to analyze each page.

An analysis trajectory is
a step-by-step analysis generated by the Step LLM that examines
the relevance of a specific external information source (e.g., a wiki

page) to the bug report, highlighting key details and their poten-
tial implications for bug reproduction. This is crucial for handling
the potential length of the pages and ensuring that only relevant
information is cited. The LLM is also instructed to disregard any
page deemed irrelevant. The resulting analysis trajectories, repre-
senting the relevant information extracted from the wiki pages, are
appended to the end of every LLM call unless otherwise specified.
Initial S2R Generation: Leveraging the bug report text, com-
ments, and curated web content, we generate an initial S2R prompt-
ing the Step LLM. This S2R is crafted to address the most critical
aspects of bug reproduction while simultaneously synthesizing the
unstructured text from the bug report and comments into a single,
structured list. Furthermore, the use of Step LLM during this gen-
eration process enhances the S2R’s effectiveness by enabling the
creation of more readily executable steps by eliminating ambiguity
and promoting the use of easier and more reliable alternate steps
compared to the original bug report.
S2R Enhancement: Following the initial one-shot generation
of the S2R, we subject it to a two-stage critique process, each stage
utilizing a separate Step LLM call. This critique specifically tar-
gets inconsistencies within the plan and the predicted behaviors
10
of in-game entities (mobs)  . The initial S2R may contain errors
regarding common mob behaviors, such as targeting, movement,
and other related factors. To address this, we employ few-shot [2]
examples to guide the Step LLM in generating suggestions for im-
provement. Subsequently, we utilize two additional Step LLM calls
to rewrite the S2R, placing emphasis on incorporating the generated
suggestions. This iterative refinement process [8] ensures the final
S2R is comprehensive, internally consistent, and accurately reflects
the expected behaviors of other agents in the game, such as mobs.
Step Clustering: The finalized S2R is then used as input for a
Step LLM prompt that instructs it to cluster the steps. This process
empirically yields 2-4 clusters, each with a descriptive title and a
list of its constituent steps. This clustering improves the readability
and digestibility of the S2R. Following cluster generation, a critique
is performed to identify any misplaced steps. Based on this critique,
steps are reordered as needed. The outcome of this process is a
refined and finalized S2R, optimized for clarity and downstream
tasks. For instance, in MC-276621, our system effectively integrated
the initial bug report and subsequent comment, enhancing the
steps to reproduce with relevant commands for easier replication
(Figure 5).
Upon completion, the Step Synthesizer yields a refined, fully
annotated S2R set, ready for execution by the Action Model.

Figure 5: Initial MC-276621 Report and Our Output
3.2.3  Action Model. The Action Model component, illustrated in
Figure 6, is responsible for translating the refined S2R into exe-
cutable scripts. These scripts interact with the game interface, en-
compassing both the main menu and in-game elements. To achieve
this, we developed a custommacro API, as existing solutions lacked
a unified approach for handling both GUI and in-game interactions.
Furthermore, the development of a custom macro API enabled the
inclusion of game-specific methods tailored for frequently used
interactions. To enhance the LLM’s ability to identify and interact
with specific GUI elements, we integrated OmniParser [7]. Omni-
Parser is, as of November 2024, a state-of-the-art model framework
for parsing UI screenshots into structured elements. It excels at
reliably identifying interactable icons and texts within the user
interface and understanding the semantics of various elements in a
screenshot while providing annotations.
3.2.3.1  Base Macro API. The custom API uses a JSON-based
REST schema, allowing the Action Model to interact with the game
environment. The API supports the following methods:
press(key: Key|List[Key], time: Optional[float]): Handles
keyboard and mouse input. If given multiple keys, it presses them
simultaneously. The optional time parameter dictates the duration
(in seconds) for which the keys are held down; if omitted, the keys
are pressed and immediately released.
write(str: string): Types the provided string into the currently
focused text box using the keyboard.

Clusters
esizer Component
command(instruction: String): ExecutesMinecraft commands.
Commands are restricted to non-menu contexts. This is the shortcut
for the LLM to open the chat, write the command, and then send
the command message.
click(coordinates: Point): Moves the mouse and clicks at the
specified coordinate.
Each JSON request can encapsulate multiple methods that are
executed sequentially. This allows for the orchestration of complex
actions that involve menu navigation, gameplay commands, and
GUI interactions. However, the click method is not directly used by
the action-taking LLM. Instead, a click_place method is provided.
This method acts as an abstraction layer, bridging the OmniParser
annotations with the API. The LLM specifies a particular bounding
box identified by OmniParser, and the click_place method trans-
lates this into a click command, using the center coordinates of
the specified bounding box.
3.2.3.2  Action Model Framework. Prior to initiating the logic
loop, the game is launched using the version specified in the bug
report, and the macro API is run in a separate thread. Finally, Om-
niParser is initialized and made ready to annotate images.
Example Trajectory Iteration
Current Step Title: Setup Minecraft Environment
Thought: We have accessed the ’More’ options menu. The
next step is to set the world type to ’Flatworld’. We need
to click on ’World’ to access the world type settings. The
correct index for ’World’ is 12.
Action: Clicked the place that had content: world. at coor-
dinates: [x1: 0.41, y1: 0.06, x2: 0.60, y2: 0.12]
Reflection: The action was successful. The screen now
shows the ’World’ options menu, which includes the
’World Type’ setting. This indicates that the ’World’ button
was clicked correctly, allowing access to the world type
settings. This matches the expected outcome, as the next
step is to set the world type to ’Flatworld’.
Classification: SUCCESS
For the ActionModel loop, we implement a logic similar to ReAct
[22]. Further details regarding the prompts utilized in the Action
Model are provided in the reproduction package. For the entire
action-taking process, we use the same MLLM, which we call the
Action LLM. To execute each S2R, the Action Model follows this
loop:

Figure 6: Action M
OmniParser Annotation: Before each action, a screenshot of
the Minecraft window is captured for annotation by OmniParser.
OmniParser analyzes the image, identifying all icons and text ele-
ments. Additionally, it classifies each element as either interactable
or non-interactable.

The resulting data is then formatted into a
structured markdown table.
Thought & Action Generation: Prior to executing an action,
the Action LLM is prompted to generate both a "thought," outlin-
ing its intended course of action, and the corresponding action
itself in a single step. This prompt is supplied with the current
trajectory (comprising previous thoughts, actions, and feedback),
the annotated table generated by OmniParser, the corresponding
game screenshot, and relevant examples. The combination of visual
input from the game image and the structured information from
the annotation table facilitates the Action LLM’s ability to identify
and interact with specific game elements.
Action Verification: Following the generation of the thought
and action, the Action LLM is prompted to verify whether the
proposed thought and action are correct [8] and conducive to pro-
gressing the game state. This verification step acts as a crucial
safeguard against errors and infinite loops, where the agent might
repeatedly execute the same ineffective action. If the verification
fails, the generated feedback, indicating the reason for failure, is
used to prompt the generation of a new thought and action pair.
This revised pair is then passed to the Macro API for execution.
Feedback Generation: After each action, a new screenshot is
taken. The Action LLM then assesses the outcome, considering the
prior thought and action. This involves judging if the thought was
achieved and if the current step cluster is nearer completion, with a
rationale. If all steps in the cluster are deemed complete, the Action
LLM can signal a move to the next. This triggers a second Action
LLM call to independently verify completion as a redundant check.
If verification fails, the active cluster stays the same.
The action model loop runs until either the game crashes or a
preset iteration limit is reached. Because the macro API logs each
action, it generates a log capable of consistently reproducing the
bug, assuming the bug isn’t dependent on randomness.
3.2.4  S2R & Action Model Implementation Details. We implement
11
the framework using Python. We use LangChain  , to implement
the LLM calling logic. For all LLM calls, we keep the temperature
at 0 to maintain maximum determinism. For the Action Model, we
only keep the last 25 iterations in Action LLM’s context to prevent
context length overflow and set the iteration limit as 30. Moreover,
while formatting the trajectory iterations, instead of putting the

3.3  Evaluation Setup
To evaluate BugCraft, we pose two research questions about its
components, considering real-world usage:
RQ1: How effective is our step synthesizer for producing correct
plans?
RQ2: How effective is BugCraft at reproducing crash-inducing bug
reports in an end-to-end manner?
To address RQ1 and RQ2, we conducted experiments using a
curated subset of our crash bug dataset. This subset comprises 100
bug reports, carefully selected to represent a variety of scenarios
that require interaction with both GUI and in-game world elements,
as mentioned in section 3.1.1. The selection of this refined dataset
was driven by the resource-intensive nature of running full evalua-
tions, making it impractical to test all selected bug reports within
the scope of this study.
3.3.1  Evaluation of the Step Synthesizer. To evaluate the accuracy
of the step clusters generated by the Step Synthesizer, we manually
reviewed each S2R. This involved attempting to reproduce the bug
inMinecraft by strictly following the generated steps. If ambiguities
or obstacles were encountered, we first consulted the original bug
report for clarification. If the original report was insufficient, we
referred to the final version of the bug report on Mojira and other
relevant online resources (e.g., Minecraft Wiki) to understand the
intended reproduction process.
Each step cluster was then labeled as either "True", "Faulty", or
"Irreproducible".
• True: Following the step cluster leads to successful bug
reproduction. Minor deviations or extra guidance steps for
the LLMs are permitted.
• Faulty: The step cluster is flawed and prevents bug repro-
duction. It may be missing crucial steps, contain incorrect
commands, or have logical inconsistencies.
• Irreproducible: The bug cannot be reproduced due to rea-
sons unrelated to the Step Synthesizer’s output.

Examples
include dependence on outdated versions, specific hard-
ware, or fundamental impossibility.
To ensure the reliability of our labeling, particularly the dis-
tinction between "Faulty" and "Irreproducible," we conducted an
inter-rater agreement analysis. Prior to the main labeling task, we.

egories and refine our understanding of the criteria. Subsequently,
we independently labeled the 100 bug reports in our run for the
step synthesizer. Inter-rater agreement was measured using both
percentage agreement and Cohen’s Kappa [9]. The results of this
analysis, including a confusion matrix, are presented 4.2.1.
Faulty step clusters were further analyzed to identify the specific
type of error. Each faulty step cluster was assigned at least one of
the following error categories:
(1) Wrong Command: An S2R is categorized here if it in-
cludes commands that are not recognized or parseable by
Minecraft. This often occurs during the S2R enhancement
phase, where the LLM may rely on its pre-trained data,
whichmight not encompass newer command syntax, result-
ing in the substitution of older, invalid command formats.
(2) Missing Step: An S2R falls under this category when it
lacks a critical step, rendering the subsequent steps implau-
sible. This issue was more prevalent with bug reports from
newer Minecraft versions. This could be attributed to the
increasing complexity of the game with each update and
the training data cutoff date of GPT-4o (October 2023)[10],
potentially leading to an incomplete understanding of the
latest game mechanics.
(3) Logic Error: This category includes steps that are impos-
sible, significantly hinder reproduction, or are internally
contradictory within the plan itself For example, simulta-
neously setting the time to day and spawning hostile mobs
(which would burn in daylight), or attempting actions re-
stricted by an open inventory screen.
To ensure the reliability of our labeling, we conducted an inter-
rater agreement analysis. Prior to the main labeling task, we jointly
inspected the results of a test run to establish the labeling cate-
gories and refine our understanding of the criteria. Subsequently,
we independently labeled the 100 bug reports in our main dataset.
Inter-rater agreement was measured using both percentage agree-
ment and Cohen’s Kappa. The results of this analysis are presented
in 4.2.
3.3.2  Evaluation of the ActionModel. To evaluate theActionModel’s
ability to reproduce bugs, we conducted a comprehensive analysis
of its performance on the subset of 86 bug reports that were deemed
reproducible during the Step Synthesizer evaluation (Section 3.3.1).
Each reproducible report was processed end-to-end, and the Action
Model’s success or failure in reproducing the bug was recorded.
3.3.3  Determining Action Model Success or Failure. The initial as-
sessment of the Action Model’s performance focused on whether
it successfully reproduced the bug or not. We classified success as
instances where the game crashed as described. Failures occurred
when no crash was observed. Due to the straightforward nature of
this assessment, a formal inter-rater agreement was not deemed
necessary.
3.3.4  Failure Analysis and Inter-Rater Agreement. To understand
the reasons behind failures, we performed a detailed examination
of each unsuccessful attempt. This involved identifying recurring
patterns and common failure modes, which informed the develop-
ment of a structured categorization rubric. Just like we did with

the Step Synthesizer rubric, we collaboratively analyzed the results
of the same test run to define the failure categories, refine our un-
derstanding of the criteria, and establish a shared understanding
of potential edge cases in applying the rubric. We also used our
understanding of common agent mistakes to enhance the rubric.
The results of this analysis, are presented in Section 4.3.
3.3.4.1  Action Model Grading Rubric. A. On Step Synthesizer
Success
(1) Success: The agent successfully reproduces the bug by
following the provided plan.
(2) Failure: The agent fails to reproduce the bug.
(a) Agent Incapability: The agent fails to reproduce the
issue due to its own limitations, despite a correct plan
and sufficient framework capabilities.
(i) Stuck in Loop: The agent enters a repetitive
loop, hindering progress.
(A) Menu Loop: The agent is trapped within
the game’s menu system, particularly the
"Create World" screen in newer versions.
(B) Command Loop: The agent repeatedly at-
tempts to execute the same incorrect or un-
parseable command.
(C) Death Loop: The agent continuously dies
due to in-game factors, such as hostilemobs,
preventing it from acting.
(ii) Poor Decision Making: The agent’s subopti-
mal choices lead to failure. This includes neglect-
ing environmental factors, skipping crucial steps,
or taking misguided actions, as detailed in the
initial category descriptions.
(b) Framework Incapability: The framework’s limita-
tions make bug reproduction impossible or extremely
difficult. This includes the inability to execute actions
within tight time constraints or to interact with spe-
cific menu elements.
(3) Error: The action model encounters an internal error and
crashes (e.g., the LLM failing to follow the JSON schema).
B.

On Step Synthesizer Failure
(1) Recovery: Despite a faulty plan from the Step Synthesizer,
the agent successfully reproduces the bug.
(2) Total Failure: Both the Step Synthesizer and the Action
Model fail to reproduce the bug.
(3) Error: The action model encounters an internal error and
crashes (e.g., Pydantic error).
4  Results
This section presents the results of our evaluation of BugCraft, fo-
cusing on the two main components: the Step Synthesizer and the
Action Model. We first analyze the Step Synthesizer’s ability to gen-
erate accurate bug reproduction plans, followed by an examination
of the Action Model’s performance in executing these plans within
the complete end-to-end pipeline.

4.1  Experimental Setup
Our experiments were conducted on a high-performance machine
running Windows 11, equipped with 32GB of RAM, an Intel i9-
14900HX CPU, and an NVIDIA RTX 4090 Mobile GPU.
We used OpenAI’s GPT-4o (specifically, the gpt-4o-2024-11-20
version) as the underlying language model for both the Step Syn-
thesizer and Action Model components. We chose this model due
to its state-of-the-art performance and widespread adoption [10].
The temperature parameter for all LLM calls was set to 0 to ensure
deterministic and consistent outputs[15].
The complete testing process for the 100 bug reports took ap-
proximately 36 hours. This duration is attributed to the inherently
sequential nature of testing each bug report, as parallelization was
not feasible. Individual runs could take up to 30 minutes, depending
on the complexity of the bug and the actions required for reproduc-
12
tion.

Table 1 summarizes the results of our Step Synthesizer evaluation,
conducted according to the methodology outlined in Section 3.3.1.
As detailed in our evaluation setup, we manually tested the gen-
erated step clusters by attempting to reproduce the bugs in the
corresponding Minecraft game versions. The step clusters were
categorized as "True" if they could successfully lead to bug repro-
duction, "Faulty" if they contained errors preventing reproduction,
or "Irreproducible" if the bug itself could not be reproduced even
by using the initial BR, which is not a fault of the step synthesizer.
The key findings, as shown in the table, are that 66.28% of the
generated step clusters were deemed "True", indicating a reason-
able level of accuracy in the Step Synthesizer’s plan generation.
However, 33.72% of the plans were categorized as "Faulty." Further
analysis of these faulty plans revealed that the most common er-
ror was "Wrong Command" (48.28%), followed by "Missing Step"
(34.49%) and "Logic Error" (31.03%). Moreover, we classified 14 bug
reports as "Irreproducible," due to factors such as dependence on
specific hardware configurations or errors in the original bug report,
underscoring the complexities of bug reproduction.
4.2.1  Inter-Rater Agreement for Step Synthesizer. To ensure the
reliability of our manual labeling of step clusters, we conducted an
inter-rater agreement analysis. The results are presented in Figure 7.

This study aimed to evaluate the BugCraft framework through
two primary research questions, focusing on the performance of
its two main components: the Step Synthesizer and the Action
Model. Our findings provide valuable insights into the capabilities
and limitations of using LLMs for automated bug reproduction in
complex game environments like Minecraft.
5.1  Addressing RQ1
The Step Synthesizer demonstrated a promising ability to trans-
form raw, unstructured bug report data into structured, actionable
steps for bug reproduction. It achieved a 66.28% success rate in
generating accurate plans (step clusters), as evaluated through the
methodology outlined in Section 3.3.1. This indicates that the com-
ponent is generally effective in understanding the reported issue,
extracting relevant information, and synthesizing it into a coherent
sequence of actions. The strong correlation between successful plan
generation and the Action Model’s success further validates the
Step Synthesizer’s performance, highlighting its crucial role in the
overall bug reproduction pipeline.
However, the 33.72% failure rate reveals that challenges remain.
The analysis of faulty plans identified three main error categories:

"Wrong Command" (48.28%), "Missing Step" (34.49%), and "Logic
Error" (31.03%). "Wrong Command" errors often stemmed from the
LLM relying on outdated command syntax from its pre-training
data, especially for newer Minecraft versions. This was particularly
evident in the LLM’s struggle to generate correct item tags, which
13
have a detailed schema that changes frequently between versions  .
Additionally, some hallucinated commands were generated that
never existed in the game. "Missing Step" errors indicated that the
Step Synthesizer sometimes failed to capture all the crucial details
needed for successful reproduction, particularly for complex bugs
or those involving recent game mechanics. This often manifested
as the LLM failing to adequately track and account for the evolving
game state. "Logic Error" cases demonstrated challenges in ensuring
the generated plan’s internal consistency and adherence to game
rules.
The Step Synthesizer offers a good foundation for bug reproduc-
tion planning, but needs improvement. It requires better integration
of version-specific game information, command verification (e.g.,
syntax parsing), and logical consistency checks. Solutions could in-
clude enhancing external data integration and incorporating game
environment feedback to refine plans dynamically.
5.2  Addressing RQ2
BugCraft achieved a 30.23% success rate in end-to-end reproduction
of crash-inducing bug reports fromour curated dataset. This demon-
strates a tangible capability for automated game bug reproduction,
showcasing the potential of LLMs to bridge the gap between nat-
ural language descriptions of bugs and concrete actions within a
complex game environment. While this success rate indicates the
feasibility of our approach, the corresponding 65.12% failure rate
highlights the inherent challenges in automating bug reproduction
within such environments.
The primary bottleneck in our end-to-end pipeline was the Ac-
tion Model. Even when provided with accurate step-by-step plans
from the Step Synthesizer, the Action Model still failed to repro-
duce the bug in a majority of cases (56.14%). This underscores the
difficulty of translating even well-defined plans into successful in-
game actions. Agent incapability was the most significant factor
hindering the Action Model’s performance. This category encom-
passed both "Poor Decision Making" (29.82%) and being "Stuck in
Loops" (19.30%). "Poor Decision Making" highlighted the challenges
faced by the Action LLM in navigating the complex, visually-rich
Minecraft environment. Operating solely on visual input, without
a deeper symbolic understanding of the game state, the LLM some-
times struggled to make optimal choices, overlooking environmen-
tal cues or deviating from the intended plan. The "Stuck in Loop"
failures further illustrated these challenges, manifesting as "Menu
Loops" (14.04%), "Command Loops" (3.51%), and even a "Death
Loop" (1.75%). "Menu Loops," particularly within the "Create World"
screen, indicated difficulties in reliably navigating complex GUI
structures. "Command Loops" arose from issues in command pars-
ing or execution, with the agent repeatedly attempting unparseable
or ineffective commands. The single "Death Loop" instance was
caused by hostile mobs continuously killing the player, preventing
progress.
13https://minecraft.wiki/w/Data_component_format

6.1  Construct Validity
Apotential threat to construct validity stems fromusing theMinecraft
Wiki for knowledge augmentation. While BugCraft might gener-
alize to other games, especially those with developer support, the
exceptionally detailed Minecraft Wiki may not be representative
of typical game documentation. This, combined with Minecraft’s
popularity, likely led to substantial Minecraft data in the LLM’s pre-
training. This pre-existing knowledge could inflate the LLM’s un-
derstanding of Minecraft, overestimating BugCraft’s performance.
Games with less documentation or popularity might see reduced
LLM effectiveness, limiting generalizability. Furthermore, the LLMs
might have been trained on the final, corrected versions of Mo-
jira bug reports, even though we used initial, unedited versions,
potentially overestimating the LLM’s ability to handle raw bug
reports.
6.2  Internal Validity
The internal validity of our study is potentially affected by the inher-
ent stochasticity of LLMs, which typically produce varying outputs
evenwith identical inputs.Whilewemitigate this by setting the tem-
perature parameter to 0, ensuring deterministic behavior in most
components, the Action Model phase presents a unique challenge.
During this phase, screenshots of the game are captured, which in-
clude Minecraft’s randomly changing, animated background. This
unavoidable game feature introduces an element of unpredictability
into the Action Model’s processing, as the visual input varies across
runs. Further testing is needed to confirm consistency between runs
since we were unable to run it multiple times due to resource and
time constraints. Furthermore, the limited number of evaluators in
the manual analysis phases could introduce bias.

Yapağcı, Öztürk and Tüzün
6.3  External Validity
The primary threat to external validity is the limited scope of our
evaluation, which focuses solely on crash bugs in Minecraft: Java
Edition. This raises concerns about the generalizability of our find-
ings to other types of bugs (e.g., functional bugs, performance
issues) and other games. Minecraft, being a sandbox game with a
vast and complex environment, may not be representative of all
game genres. Furthermore, the specific mechanics and structure
of Minecraft: Java Edition, such as the inclusion of an extensive
scripting system, might not be comparable to other games. There-
fore, while BugCraft demonstrates promising results within the
Minecraft context, caution should be exercised when extrapolating
these findings to other games or bug types. Further research involv-
ing diverse games and bug categories is necessary to establish the
broader applicability of our framework. Additionally, our reliance
on publicly available bug reports from Mojira might introduce a
bias, as these reports may not fully represent the issues encountered
by the entire player base.

The paper introduces the BugCraft-Bench, a novel benchmark for
Minecraft bug reproduction, and BugCraft, an LLM-based frame-
work for end-to-end crash bug reproduction inMinecraft. BugCraft-
Bench addresses the dataset gap in game bug reproduction research,
offering a curated set of 86 Minecraft crash bug reports for standard-
ized evaluation. BugCraft framework achieves a 30.23% end-to-end
success rate in reproducing crash bugs, with its Step Synthesizer
demonstrating 66.28% accuracy in plan generation, highlighting
LLMs’ potential for automating game bug reproduction. However,
limitations in the Action Model, such as the agent getting stuck in
loops and poor decision making by the agent were identified, along
with Step Synthesizer challenges in command accuracy and detail
completeness, especially for newer Minecraft versions.
Moving forward, several key areas warrant further investigation.
Future work should focus on enhancing LLM game environment
understanding, potentially through collaboration with game devel-
opers to create specialized APIs for enhanced in-game movement
and interaction, similar to the high-level API Voyager[19] uses,
called Mineflayer[13]. This could also improve bug reproduction
across numerous game versions, addressing the limitations of our
current vision-based approach. Furthermore, developing compre-
hensive datasets and robust ground truth verification methods, par-
ticularly for non-crash bugs, are crucial. Future benchmarks should
incorporate automated or semi-automated ground truth methods to
evaluate a wider range of bug types. Assessing BugCraft’s general-
izability beyond Minecraft is also essential, requiring investigation
into its adaptability to diverse game engines, genres, and complexi-
ties through comparative studies. Our roadmap includes improving
BugCraft based on identified weaknesses, including addressing chal-
lenges with the Action Model and Step Synthesizer. We also plan to
extend the agent to handle multiplayer bugs and bugs involving file
loading (Worlds, datapacks, world setting JSONs). Ultimately, the
advancements presented in this paper provide a strong foundation
for future advancements in automated bug reproduction, paving
the way for more automatic game development processes.
